acasus.name: Acasus
acasus.sub_archetype_detail: Mid-size, founder-led consulting firm delivering implementation (not just advice) for public sector reform in LMICs. Revenue from government/multilateral contracts. Differentiates on field presence, local talent, and measurable outcomes.

lookalikes.name: Oxford Policy Management (OPM)
lookalikes.detail: Research-driven development consultancy. 500+ staff. LMIC policy advisory and implementation. Overlaps on evidence-based reform.

lookalikes.name: Dalberg
lookalikes.detail: Impact advisory firm. 700+ staff across 25 offices. Strategy consulting for social impact. More strategy, less embedded delivery.

lookalikes.name: Adam Smith International
lookalikes.detail: Government reform advisory. 2,000+ staff. Heavy FCDO/USAID contract base. Larger, more traditional contractor.

lookalikes.name: Results for Development (R4D)
lookalikes.detail: Non-profit think tank + advisory. Health and education systems. Evidence-based. Smaller, more research-oriented.

lookalikes.name: IDinsight
lookalikes.detail: Data analytics for social impact. 200+ staff. Uses data science for development decisions. Strong analytics parallel to Acasus Budapest hub.

lookalikes.name: Palladium
lookalikes.detail: Large-scale development management. 4,000+ staff. More program management than reform delivery. Competes for same donor contracts.

lookalikes.name: DAI
lookalikes.detail: International development company. 5,000+ staff. Strong USAID relationships. Traditional development contractor at larger scale.

lookalikes.name: Chemonics
lookalikes.detail: Largest USAID contractor by volume. 5,000+ staff. Supply chain and health systems. Different model but same donor ecosystem.

lookalikes.name: Abt Associates
lookalikes.detail: Research + implementation in health, education, governance. 3,500+ staff. More US government focused than Acasus.

lookalikes.name: Clinton Health Access Initiative (CHAI)
lookalikes.detail: Health systems strengthening in LMICs. Non-profit. 2,000+ staff. Overlaps on public health reform delivery. Different funding model.

common_pain_points.pain: Talent pipeline for field-based LMIC specialists
common_pain_points.detail: Every firm in this archetype struggles to recruit people with the rare combination of field experience, analytical skills, and willingness to work in challenging environments.

common_pain_points.pain: Donor funding volatility and contract lumpiness
common_pain_points.detail: Revenue depends on aid budget cycles. US foreign aid contraction, FCDO freezes, and EU budget politics create systemic risk.
Field teams generate institutional knowledge that gets lost between projects. Methodologies, data, and relationships are trapped in individuals.
Government procurement requires lengthy proposals. Win rates are 10-25%. Significant staff time devoted to business development.
Clients demand proof of outcomes but causation is hard to establish. Data collection in LMICs is patchy. RCTs are expensive.
Key client relationships often sit with individuals. Founder/partner transitions can destabilize business development pipeline.

cspd.name: Catholic Schools Parramatta Diocese
cspd.sub_archetype_detail: Large-scale Catholic diocesan school systems operating K-12 networks with government funding dependency, mission-driven governance, and system-wide pedagogical frameworks.

lookalikes.name: Melbourne Archdiocese Catholic Schools (MACS)
lookalikes.detail: Largest Catholic diocesan system in Australia. Comparable governance structure (archdiocese-operated, not independent), K-12 systemic schools, government-funded. Facing the same pedagogical transformation pressures — moved to explicit instruction system-wide in 2024, directly analogous to CSPD's Uplift launch in 2025. Key initiatives: ["MACS 2030 strategic plan ('Forming Lives to Enrich the World') launched 2022 — four pillars: Inspired by Faith, Flourishing Learners, Enabled Leaders, Enriched Communities", 'Vision for Instruction (VFI) launched February 2024 — system-wide switch to explicit instruction for literacy and numeracy across all 291 primary schools; early adopters showing NAPLAN improvements of up to 46 percentage points', 'Vision for Engagement (VFE) framework — addresses screen time, student wellbeing and conditions for learning', 'SAP S/4HANA Cloud + SAP SuccessFactors ERP transformation via Infosys — announced July 2025, consolidating HR, finance, and operations across ~300 schools onto a single cloud platform with AI readiness as an explicit goal']

lookalikes.name: Sydney Catholic Schools (SCS)
lookalikes.detail: Direct NSW Catholic peer operating under the same CSNSW umbrella. Governed and funded on the same state/commonwealth model. Neighbouring diocese — shares the NSW Enterprise Agreement, NESA accreditation framework, and CSNSW shared services. Key point of differentiation: SCS is significantly larger (~74,000 students, 147 schools) and serves more affluent communities (inner and south Sydney vs. western Sydney). Key initiatives: ["Magis 2033 strategic plan launched early 2026 — decade-long roadmap with three focus areas: 'Integral Formation for Human Excellence' (core), 'Schooling Excellence Accessible to All', 'Stewardship Excellence for Mission'; developed with Boston College researchers", "School reporting overhaul announced February 2026 — ED Danielle Cronin declared parents 'short-changed' by current reports; new framework to assess creativity, discernment, resilience, practical wisdom alongside academic results; questions twice-yearly reporting cycle", 'CSNSW-level data and analytics platform (joint with all NSW dioceses) — Azure Databricks + Power BI centralising data from 257,000 students across 11 dioceses, saving 6-8 weeks annually previously spent on data reconciliation']

lookalikes.name: Brisbane Catholic Education (BCE)
lookalikes.detail: Comparable scale Catholic diocesan system in a major Australian city. Faces same teacher burnout and workload pressures. Most advanced Catholic diocesan system in Australia on AI deployment — their M365 Copilot rollout is the largest K-12 generative AI deployment globally, and their AI-assisted teaching pilot showed measurable NAPLAN gains. Key initiatives: ['Microsoft 365 Copilot deployment to 12,500 educators and support staff — largest K-12 Copilot rollout globally; phased Jan 2025 to 2026; trial at St Francis College Crestmead showed average 9.3 hours saved per teacher per week', "Custom AI chatbot ('Copilot Studio' grounded in Catholic values) deployed system-wide across 146 schools — first sector-wide generative AI deployment in a non-government school system in NSW/QLD", 'AI-assisted teaching pilot — 8 pilot schools grew NAPLAN scores from 490 to 555 points (2023 to 2024); Year 3 improved 7.22% while state average fell 0.57%', "BCE Education Strategy 2025-2027 — emphasis on 'transformational experiences' via emerging technology and evidence-based teaching; 1,900 inputs from staff and 100+ student voices"]

lookalikes.name: Catholic Education Canberra-Goulburn (CECG)
lookalikes.detail: Smaller Catholic diocesan system (56 schools) but the most directly instructive peer for CSPD's Uplift framework. CECG launched the Catalyst explicit instruction program in 2020 — five years ahead of CSPD — and has published externally evaluated, measurable outcomes (Deloitte Access Economics evaluation). CSPD's new EGM Learning Outcomes Patrick Ellis previously held an Education Lead role at CECG, making this the closest methodological ancestor of Uplift. Key initiatives: ['Catalyst program launched 2020 — system-wide explicit instruction and High Impact Teaching Practice (HITP) rollout; 5,400+ professional learning units completed; 5,400+ curriculum resources developed with Ochre Education for primary English and Mathematics', 'Deloitte Access Economics external evaluation (2020-2023) — established baseline and tracked three-year impact; findings published 2024', "Measurable NAPLAN outcomes: 75% of CECG students assessed as proficient readers in 2024 vs national average of 66%; 13 of top 20 ACT schools 'making the most difference' (ACARA 2023/24) were CECG schools", 'Teacher time savings: teachers report saving up to 6 hours per week through structured curriculum resources; 4 in 5 teachers recommend the Catalyst approach to colleagues']

lookalikes.name: Catholic Education Western Australia (CEWA)
lookalikes.detail: Large Catholic diocesan system with the most advanced data and digital infrastructure investment among Australian Catholic systems. CEWA's LEADing Lights platform (student analytics, predictive intervention) and Microsoft 365 migration represent the most mature digital operating model in Catholic education nationally. CSPD elevated its CIO role in November 2023 — CEWA demonstrates what a mature next-state looks like. Key initiatives: ['LEADing Lights platform (Learning, Engagement, Accountability, Discipleship) — single Microsoft cloud platform integrating HR, finance, attendance, student performance and wellbeing data across all 164 schools; enables principal-level dashboards with real-time NAPLAN and WACE benchmarking', 'Predictive student intervention: platform designed to flag early warning signals (unusual absence patterns, unexpected assessment drops) for individual students and trigger support actions', 'Microsoft 365 global data science partnership — three-year research collaboration on teaching effectiveness; 90,000+ M365 accounts, 1PB+ data under management', 'Network upgrade: dual NBN fibre + 4G into every school; connection speeds increased 400%+ to ensure equitable digital access for regional/remote students', 'Freshservice IT service management platform deployed across 164 schools — unified ticketing, asset lifecycle management (Jamf integration), and self-service portal; previously fragmented email-based helpdesk']

ipeople.name: iPeople Inc
ipeople.sub_archetype_detail: Publicly listed or PE-backed holding company operating multiple higher education institutions in a high-growth emerging market. Revenue from tuition, funded by parent conglomerate or PE. Scale via acquisition and organic enrollment growth.

lookalikes.name: PHINMA Education
lookalikes.detail: Direct competitor. 177,851 students. KKR-backed. Provincial affordable HE. Larger, higher margins, different geographic focus.

lookalikes.name: Centro Universitario (CEUNI/Yduqs)
lookalikes.detail: Brazilian private HE group. 1.6M+ students. Listed (B3). Acquisition-led growth in affordable tertiary education.

lookalikes.name: Cogna Educacao
lookalikes.detail: Largest private education company in Brazil. 1.5M+ students. K-12 + HE. Content + operations. Listed (B3).

lookalikes.name: Manipal Education
lookalikes.detail: Multi-campus HE group. 400K+ students across India, Malaysia, Antigua. Medical + engineering focus.

lookalikes.name: Amity Education Group
lookalikes.detail: Large Indian private university network. 150K+ students. Multi-campus across India and international.

lookalikes.name: Ama Education System
lookalikes.detail: Philippine IT-focused HE network. Direct competitor in tech education. Broader geographic spread.

lookalikes.name: STI Education Systems
lookalikes.detail: Listed (PSE:STI). ICT and business vocational education. Different tier but same market.

lookalikes.name: Anima Educacao
lookalikes.detail: Brazilian private HE. 200K+ students. Premium positioning. Listed. Acquisition-led growth.

lookalikes.name: Sharda University
lookalikes.detail: Multi-campus Indian university. STEM + medical focus. Growing through branch campuses.

lookalikes.name: INTI International University (Laureate)
lookalikes.detail: Malaysian private HE. Part of former Laureate network. Regional private HE model in SEA.

common_pain_points.detail: Each acquired institution has different systems, culture, and quality levels. Standardizing without losing local identity is the central operational challenge.
Government tuition caps (CHED in Philippines, MEC in Brazil) limit pricing power. Cost discipline is essential but constrains investment.

common_pain_points.pain: Board exam / accreditation performance as brand driver
common_pain_points.detail: In regulated HE markets, pass rates on licensure exams are the primary quality signal. Directly drives enrollment. Hard to improve across many campuses.

common_pain_points.pain: Faculty quality and retention at scale
common_pain_points.detail: Licensed faculty (especially healthcare, engineering) are scarce in emerging markets. Salary pressure from competitors and private practice.
Acquired institutions often have minimal IT. Enterprise SIS, LMS, and data systems need to be deployed across 5-20 heterogeneous campuses.
Conglomerate/PE owners expect returns. Students expect affordability. Balancing financial performance with mission is constant tension.

kinetic.name: Kinetic Software
kinetic.sub_archetype: Vertical SaaS for Higher Education Operations
kinetic.sub_archetype_detail: Niche B2B SaaS company selling to universities and colleges. Focuses on non-academic operations: accommodation, facilities, conferencing, student services. Revenue from subscriptions + implementation. 50-200 employees, dominant in one geography.

lookalikes.name: StarRez
lookalikes.detail: Direct competitor. Student accommodation software. 1,400 customers, 3M+ beds. Dominates North America and Australia. The other half of the global duopoly.

lookalikes.name: Tribal Group
lookalikes.detail: UK-based HE management software. Student records, admissions, fees. 400+ customers. Different functional focus but same buyer.

lookalikes.name: Unit4
lookalikes.detail: ERP for service-centric organizations including universities. Finance, HR, student management. Larger, more horizontal.

lookalikes.name: Ellucian
lookalikes.detail: Largest HE-specific software company. SIS, ERP, CRM for 2,700+ institutions. Market leader in academic administration. Different functional layer.

lookalikes.name: Civitas Learning
lookalikes.detail: Student success analytics for HE. Predictive analytics for retention. Different function but same institutional buyer and data integration challenge.

lookalikes.name: Symplicity
lookalikes.detail: Student affairs and career services software. Campus conduct, disability, housing. Overlaps on student services operations.

lookalikes.name: Anthology (Blackboard)
lookalikes.detail: Large HE platform (LMS + SIS + analytics). Different primary function (academic) but expanding into operations.

lookalikes.name: Concord (accommodation)
lookalikes.detail: UK student accommodation management software. Smaller, direct competitor in UK market. Kinetic has largely won this competition.

lookalikes.name: Planet eStream / Panopto
lookalikes.detail: HE-focused SaaS in content/media. Same buyer persona (university IT/operations). Shows the vertical SaaS for HE pattern.

lookalikes.name: Kx (student engagement)
lookalikes.detail: Student engagement and wellbeing platforms for HE. Adjacent vertical. Same institutional procurement process.

common_pain_points.pain: Geographic expansion beyond saturated home market
common_pain_points.detail: Vertical SaaS leaders hit 60-90% penetration in home geography. International expansion requires different sales motion, regulatory compliance, and product localization.
Expand into adjacent modules (Kinetic: accommodation + conferencing + catering + engagement) or go deeper in core? Small teams cant do both.

common_pain_points.pain: Enterprise sales cycle in public sector
common_pain_points.detail: University procurement is slow (6-18 months), framework-driven, and committee-based. High CAC relative to deal size.
Universities run decades-old SIS, HR, and finance systems. Every customer needs different integration work. Professional services burden.

common_pain_points.pain: Platform risk from horizontal SaaS players
common_pain_points.detail: Large platforms (Yardi, Oracle, SAP) could move into education vertical. Vertical SaaS moat is domain expertise, not technology.

common_pain_points.pain: Scaling professional services without killing margins
common_pain_points.detail: Enterprise HE customers need implementation, training, and ongoing support. Services revenue has lower margins than subscriptions.

phinma.name: PHINMA Education
phinma.sub_archetype_detail: Large-scale operator of affordable higher education institutions in emerging markets, growing through disciplined acquisition of underperforming schools and turnaround. Revenue from tuition + government subsidies. PE-backed or public. 100K+ students.

lookalikes.name: Cogna Educacao
lookalikes.detail: 1.5M+ students. Largest private education company in Brazil. Acquisition-led growth. Listed. Content + operations + K-12.

lookalikes.name: Yduqs (Estacio)
lookalikes.detail: 1.6M+ students. Brazilian HE group. Acquisition-led. Distance learning + campus. Direct model parallel.

lookalikes.name: Ser Educacional
lookalikes.detail: 300K+ students. Northeast Brazil focus (underserved). Acquisition model targeting provincial markets. Closest parallel to PHINMA.

lookalikes.name: Anima Educacao
lookalikes.detail: 200K+ students. Premium + affordable tiers. Acquisition-led. Listed on B3. Growing margins through standardization.

lookalikes.name: Manipal Education
lookalikes.detail: 400K+ students. Multi-campus Indian HE group. Medical + engineering. Different market but same operating model.

lookalikes.name: NIIT University / NIIT Learning
lookalikes.detail: Tech-focused education in India. Growing through partnerships and campuses. Smaller scale but same acquisition logic.

lookalikes.name: Ama Education System
lookalikes.detail: IT-focused Philippine HE network. Direct domestic competitor. Broader geographic spread but lower prestige.

lookalikes.name: Adtalem Global Education
lookalikes.detail: US healthcare-focused HE. Chamberlain University (nursing), Ross Medical. Parallels PHINMA nursing focus.

lookalikes.name: Strategic Education (Capella/Strayer)
lookalikes.detail: US online HE focused on working adults. Different delivery but same affordable-accessible-employment-outcome model.

lookalikes.name: Universitas Ciputra
lookalikes.detail: Indonesian private university. Entrepreneurship focus. Potential competitor in PHINMA Indonesia expansion.

common_pain_points.detail: Acquired schools are underperforming by definition. Standardizing operations, upgrading faculty, fixing facilities, and improving outcomes while keeping costs low is the central challenge.
Licensed professionals (nursing, engineering, criminology) prefer urban practice over provincial teaching. Structural shortage limits growth.
Free HE Act (Philippines), PROUNI (Brazil), reservation quotas (India). Revenue floors depend on government policy stability.
Each acquired school has different (or no) IT systems. Deploying unified SIS, LMS, and operational systems across 10-20+ campuses.

common_pain_points.pain: Quality consistency measured by board exam pass rates
common_pain_points.detail: Licensure exams are the primary quality signal. One underperforming campus can damage network brand. Monitoring and intervention across many sites.
Different regulatory environments (CHED vs Indonesian Dikti). JV structures. Currency risk. Cultural differences in education delivery.

rising.name: Rising Academies
rising.sub_archetype_detail: Mission-driven organization operating or supporting schools in Sub-Saharan Africa, with government partnerships and technology-enabled delivery. Revenue from grants, impact investment, and government contracts. Focus on measurable learning outcomes.

lookalikes.name: NewGlobe
lookalikes.detail: Largest SSA school management platform. Government partnerships in Nigeria (EdoBEST), Liberia, India. Scripted curriculum + tech. More scale, more controversy.

lookalikes.name: Bridge International Academies
lookalikes.detail: Low-cost private school chain. Pioneer of the model. Political backlash in Kenya/Uganda. Rising differentiates on evidence + B-Corp.

lookalikes.name: BRAC Education (Bangladesh)
lookalikes.detail: World largest NGO-run education program. 1M+ students. Non-formal + formal schools. Different geography but same mission-driven model.

lookalikes.name: Pratham
lookalikes.detail: Indias largest education NGO. ASER (Annual Status of Education Report) creator. Evidence-driven. Overlaps on measurement rigor.

lookalikes.name: Teach For All / Teach First
lookalikes.detail: Global teacher recruitment/training network. Overlaps on teacher quality improvement. Different model (placement vs operation).

lookalikes.name: Lively Minds
lookalikes.detail: Early childhood education in Ghana and Uganda. RCT-validated. Government partnership model. Smaller, earlier stage.

lookalikes.name: Eneza Education
lookalikes.detail: Mobile learning platform for SSA. SMS + app tutoring. Overlaps on mobile delivery channel. Less school-level integration.

lookalikes.name: Ubongo
lookalikes.detail: Edutainment media for SSA. TV + mobile. 30M+ learners reached. Content approach vs Rising operational model.

lookalikes.name: IDP Foundation (Rising spin-off investor)
lookalikes.detail: Impact investor in African education. Funded Rising. Portfolio includes other SSA education ventures.

lookalikes.name: Omega Schools (Ghana)
lookalikes.detail: Low-cost private school chain in Ghana. Pay-as-you-learn model. Smaller. Different funding model but same market.

common_pain_points.pain: Government payment delays and political risk
common_pain_points.detail: B2G contracts in SSA = budget cycle dependency, political turnover risk, and 3-12 month payment delays. Cash flow management is existential.

common_pain_points.pain: Quality consistency across hundreds of sites
common_pain_points.detail: Operating 100-1,000+ schools across multiple countries with limited infrastructure, connectivity, and management bandwidth.

common_pain_points.pain: Teacher quality and retention in rural areas
common_pain_points.detail: Qualified teachers prefer urban employment. Rural/remote school staffing depends on local recruitment with intensive training.
Transitioning from grant-funded to earned revenue (government contracts, fee-based services) without losing mission focus.
WhatsApp works but internet is unreliable. Offline-first design, SMS fallbacks, and low-bandwidth optimization are constant constraints.
Donors and governments demand proof of learning outcomes. RCTs are expensive. Routine assessment data collection in SSA is unreliable.

school-for-life.name: School for Life Foundation
school-for-life.sub_archetype_detail: Small to mid-size charity headquartered in a wealthy country (Australia, UK, US), raising funds through events and major donors, operating schools directly in a single developing country. Deep community engagement, founder-led, 5-20 schools.
defining_traits: Dual-geography (fundraise in West, operate in South)

lookalikes.name: PEAS (Promoting Equality in African Schools)
lookalikes.detail: 35 secondary schools in Uganda and Zambia. UK charity. Direct model parallel. Larger, more institutional funding.

lookalikes.name: Educate! (Uganda)
lookalikes.detail: Youth skills and entrepreneurship education in Uganda, Rwanda, Kenya. Similar geography. More government integration.

lookalikes.name: One Girl
lookalikes.detail: Australian charity for girls education in Sierra Leone. Same donor base (Australian women). Different country, similar model.

lookalikes.name: The School Fund
lookalikes.detail: US-based, funds schools in East Africa. Smaller. Direct scholarship model rather than school operation.

lookalikes.name: BuildOn
lookalikes.detail: US charity building schools in developing countries. Different model (build not operate). Same event-driven fundraising.

lookalikes.name: Opportunity International (Education Finance)
lookalikes.detail: Microfinance for school fees in developing countries. Australian office. Some donor base overlap.

lookalikes.name: Asante Africa Foundation
lookalikes.detail: US-based charity operating education programs in East Africa (Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania). Similar geography and model.

lookalikes.name: African Revival
lookalikes.detail: UK charity supporting schools in Uganda and Zambia. 300+ schools supported. Similar geography, different scale.

lookalikes.name: Children in Crossfire
lookalikes.detail: Education charity operating in Tanzania and Ethiopia. Event-driven fundraising. Founder-led.

lookalikes.name: Empower Africa
lookalikes.detail: Australian charity supporting education and health in East Africa. Same donor ecosystem. Event-driven model.

common_pain_points.pain: Revenue concentration in events and major donors
common_pain_points.detail: Gala balls, dinners, and a small number of HNW individuals drive 60-80% of revenue. One bad year or donor departure is material.
Founder personal brand is the primary fundraising asset. Organizational sustainability beyond the founder is the existential question.
Revenue in AUD/GBP/USD, costs in local currency (UGX, KES, TZS). Currency swings directly affect operational capacity.
Donors want stories AND data. Small charities lack resources for rigorous M&E. Balancing narrative with evidence is constant.
HQ team of 10-30 managing field team of 100-300 across time zones, cultures, and infrastructure gaps. Communication and oversight challenges.
Growth (more schools) requires more institutional funding. Institutional funders demand different reporting, governance, and professionalization than event-based donors.

wise.name: WISE
wise.sub_archetype_detail: Education or development-focused convening organization backed by sovereign wealth or state foundation. Operates global summits, prizes, research programs, and community networks. Value measured in influence and network, not revenue. 20-100 FTE.

lookalikes.name: World Economic Forum (WEF)
lookalikes.detail: Global convening platform. Davos summit. Much larger but same model: sovereign/corporate backing + global influence mission. Gold standard for the archetype.

lookalikes.name: Aga Khan Foundation (Education)
lookalikes.detail: Education programs + convening via Aga Khan Development Network. Sovereign-backed (Ismaili community). Schools + policy + research.

lookalikes.name: Brookings Institution (Education)
lookalikes.detail: Think tank with major education policy program (Center for Universal Education). Research + convening. Different funding model (endowment + grants).

lookalikes.name: HundrED
lookalikes.detail: Education innovation platform. Identifies and shares innovations. Finnish credibility. Smaller, less political clout than WISE.

lookalikes.name: GEMS Education Foundation
lookalikes.detail: Gulf-based education foundation (GEMS group). Overlaps on Gulf education ecosystem. More operator-focused than WISE.

lookalikes.name: ASU+GSV Summit
lookalikes.detail: Largest EdTech investment summit. 7,000+ attendees. US-centric, commercially focused. WISE is policy-focused, Global South.

lookalikes.name: UNESCO IIEP
lookalikes.detail: UN education planning institute. Research + capacity building. Institutional authority. More bureaucratic than WISE.

lookalikes.name: Global Partnership for Education (GPE)
lookalikes.detail: Multilateral fund for education in developing countries. $7B+ deployed. Funding mechanism vs convening, but overlapping stakeholders.

lookalikes.name: Varkey Foundation
lookalikes.detail: Gulf-based education foundation. Global Teacher Prize ($1M). Direct parallel to WISE Prize model. Same regional ecosystem.

lookalikes.name: Salzburg Global Seminar
lookalikes.detail: Global convening platform across sectors including education. Fellowship model. European rather than Gulf-backed. Same archetype pattern.

common_pain_points.detail: When one entity provides ~100% of funding, strategic priorities must align with funder leadership. Organizational independence is constrained.
How do you prove a summit changed policy? ROI metrics (attendees, media mentions, community size) are weak proxies for actual influence.
Summits happen biannually or annually. Maintaining community engagement, research output, and brand presence in between is resource-intensive.
20-100 FTE cannot produce research, manage community, organize events, run prizes, AND maintain policy influence simultaneously.
Sovereign backing (Gulf states, China, etc.) creates perception challenges. Balancing funder interests with credibility among Western/Global South audiences.
Mission-driven staff but limited career progression in a 20-100 person organization. Burnout from global scope on small team.
